coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on coumarin dye-functionalized carbon dots for bisulfite detection in living cells and food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qin
Sun, Yanli
Ge, Ju
Li, Lihua
Lu, Jiufu
Zhang, Dan
Jin, Lingxia
Li, Hua
Zhang, Shengrui
coredata.dc:description: As an important food additive, extensive intake of bisulfite (HSO3 −) often leads to serious related diseases. However, the reliability and accuracy of HSO3 − detection remains an outstanding challenge because the majority of previously reported sensors for HSO3 − have been evaluated by the single-channel signal output. Herein, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe, Coum-CDs, by covalently coupling the coumarin dye (Coum) onto the surface of yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) via amide bonds. The Coum-CDs themselves showed peculiar dual fluorescence emission at 480 and 565 nm under single-wavelength excitation. However, upon treatment with HSO3 −, the π-conjugation structure of Coum was destroyed due to the selectively nucleophilic reaction between the C-4 atom of Coum and HSO3 −, which shut off the pathway of fluorescence resonance energy transfer from Coum to Y-CDs. As a result, the fluorescence emission of Coum-CDs at 565 nm decreased significantly, accompanied by a slight change in fluorescence intensity at 480 nm, allowing the ratiometric detection of HSO3 −. In addition, the nanoprobe Coum-CDs displays a very low detection limit of 15 nM and excellent selectivity without interference from other relevant analytes, especially reactive sulfur-containing species. Importantly, preliminary experiments showed that the Coum-CDs have been successfully applied to ratiometric monitoring of exogenous and endogenous HSO3 − in living cells, as well as the quantitative measurement of HSO3 − in sugar and wine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric nanoprobe
Bisulfite
Bioimaging
Food samples

Characterization and optical behaviors of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs
Ratiometric sensing property of Coum-CDs for HSO3 −
Interference study of the Coum-CD nanoprobe
The sensor Coum-CDs detect HSO3 − in real food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22177066 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2021KJXX-51 ), the Educational Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Province of China ( 20JK0575 ), and the Co-construction Project of Hanzhong city and Shaanxi University of Technology ( SXJ-2103 , SXJ-2105 ), the Youth Innovative Team of Shaanxi Universities [No.2022.94].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the structure and functional groups of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs. (A) XPS survey; (B) FT-IR characterization; high-resolution XPS survey scan of C 1 s (C, D), N 1 s (E, F) spectra of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs.

Absorption (A) and fluorescence (B) spectral changes of Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) upon addition of HSO3 − in PB solution (pH 7.4, containing 2% acetone as a cosolvent) excited at 350 nm; (C) Selectivity of Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) toward HSO3 − (20 μM) and other relevant species (species concentration, 500 μM) in 20 mM PB solution (pH 7.4, containing 2% acetone as a cosolvent).

HeLa cells were incubated with Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h and then with HSO3 − at different concentrations (0, 1 and 10 μM) for 0.5 h. Images were acquired using 405 nm excitation and emission channels of 450–490 nm (blue) and 510–650 nm (yellow). (b) Fluorescence intensity quantitation was analyzed by ImageJ.

(a) The first row (vertically): HeLa cells were incubated with Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h; the second row: HeLa cells were incubated with Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h, and then with 500 mM GSH and 250 mM Na2S2O3 for 0.5 h; the third row: HeLa cells were incubated with Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h, then with 10 mM TNBS for 0.5 h, followed by 500 mM GSH and 250 mM Na2S2O3 for another 0.5 h; the fourth row: HeLa cells were incubated with Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h, then with 500 mM GSH for another 0.5 h. (b) From left to right: the relative ratio of yellow/blue fluorescence intensity of rows 1, 2, 3 and 4 in (a). The ratio images were all obtained as I yellow /I blue. Images were acquired from 450 to 490 nm for blue fluorescence, and from 510 to 650 nm for yellow fluorescence. λex = 405 nm.
Preparation of the nanoprobe Coum-CDs and schematic illustration of ratiometric detection for HSO3 −.
A comparison of nanomaterial-based methods for detection of HSO3 − value.
Determination of the concentration of HSO3 − in real food samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on coumarin dye-functionalized carbon dots for bisulfite detection in living cells and food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important food additive, extensive intake of bisulfite (HSO3 −) often leads to serious related diseases. However, the reliability and accuracy of HSO3 − detection remains an outstanding challenge because the majority of previously reported sensors for HSO3 − have been evaluated by the single-channel signal output. Herein, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe, Coum-CDs, by covalently coupling the coumarin dye (Coum) onto the surface of yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) via amide bonds. The Coum-CDs themselves showed peculiar dual fluorescence emission at 480 and 565 nm under single-wavelength excitation. However, upon treatment with HSO3 −, the π-conjugation structure of Coum was destroyed due to the selectively nucleophilic reaction between the C-4 atom of Coum and HSO3 −, which shut off the pathway of fluorescence resonance energy transfer from Coum to Y-CDs. As a result, the fluorescence emission of Coum-CDs at 565 nm decreased significantly, accompanied by a slight change in fluorescence intensity at 480 nm, allowing the ratiometric detection of HSO3 −. In addition, the nanoprobe Coum-CDs displays a very low detection limit of 15 nM and excellent selectivity without interference from other relevant analytes, especially reactive sulfur-containing species. Importantly, preliminary experiments showed that the Coum-CDs have been successfully applied to ratiometric monitoring of exogenous and endogenous HSO3 − in living cells, as well as the quantitative measurement of HSO3 − in sugar and wine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisulfite (HSO3 −) is an essential food additive that is extensively used to preserve food by preventing decay and oxidation reactions caused by microorganisms [1]. However, if the concentration of HSO3 − exceeds the safe value range, it is considered to be toxic, and exposure to it could result in asthma attacks and other allergic reactions [2,3]. Because of the aforementioned factors, the concentration of HSO3 − allowed in food has been strictly regulated, and the food and life sciences are currently focusing on the selective detection and precise quantification of HSO3 −. To date, several fluorescent probes have been generated to detect HSO3 − by taking advantage of a variety of response reaction mechanisms, such as nucleophilic addition with electron-deficient “CC” bonds, aldehyde, ketone, or CN isomerization inhibition groups [4–7]. The majority of these probes have been tested using single-channel signal output, which may reduce the accuracy of the measured results. Although these known fluorescence probes for HSO3 − show relatively good selectivity, the method's accuracy needs to be improved before dedicated HSO3 − probes can be developed for practical applications [8–11].

Taking this into consideration, much effort has been focused on constructing ratiometric fluorescent probes to sense HSO3 −-induced fluctuation [12–19]. Despite the improved accuracy and signal-to-noise ratio, fluorescent probes with obvious advantages, such as uncomplicated synthetic steps and chemical inertness, are still lacking for HSO3 − detection. Therefore, much effort has been focused on developing nanomaterial-based ratiometric fluorescent probes for monitoring HSO3 − in biological and food samples. Among these nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) can be molecularly engineered as unprecedented chemosensors by functionalizing organic molecules with specific reaction sites on the surface of CDs [20–25]. For example, Huang et al. reported a fluorescent “turn-on” nanoprobe to determine HSO3 − through molecular engineering of fluorescent CDs by a cyanine dye based on a 1,4-addition reaction [26]. Chen et al. rationally constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing HSO3 − by introducing aldehyde as the response group on the surface of rCDs [27]. However, CD-based fluorescent nanoprobes suitable for monitoring HSO3 − are still rare. To address this issue, designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on CDs for HSO3 − is needed.

Motivated by these needs, we rationally designed and synthesized an unprecedented ratiometric nanoprobe, Coum-CDs, by covalently coupling the coumarin dye (Coum) onto the surface of yellow emissive CDs (Y-CDs) with amide bonds for selective and quantitative testing of HSO3 − based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The Y-CDs were used as the acceptor of energy transfer and carrier for the sensing system, and Coum was used as the energy donor and HSO3 − recognition element. The Coum-CDs displayed intrinsic dual fluorescence emission at 480 and 565 nm. However, upon treatment with HSO3 −, the fluorescence emission of Coum-CDs at 565 nm decreased significantly, accompanied by a slight change in fluorescence intensity at 480 nm, allowing the ratiometric detection of HSO3 −. This new HSO3 − nanoprobe is promising because of its higher sensitivity and outstanding specificity toward HSO3 − without interference from other relevant analytes, especially reactive sulfur-containing species. Taking advantage of its attractive sensing property, the Coum-CDs sensor was smoothly applied to detect exogenous and endogenous HSO3 − in living cells. In addition, when using Coum-CDs for the test of HSO3 − in sugar and wine, the recoveries of measurements are desirable.

Unless otherwise specified, all of the chemicals used in this investigation were purchased from various commercial distributors, and no additional purification was necessary for their use. The entire experiment was conducted with distilled water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a GZF2.0 FEI Electron Optics (Thermo Scientific, America https://www.thermofisher.com/). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was undertaken using an ESCALAB 250Xi multi-technique surface analyzer (Thermo Scientific, America https:// https://www.thermofisher.com/). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany https://www.bruker.com/) with KBr pellets. The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan https://www.shimadzu.com). A fluorescence spectrometer model RF5301(Shimadzu, Japan https://www.shimadzu.com) was employed for the examination of the spectra of fluorescence. 1H NMR spectra were recorded at 400 MHz on an INOVA-400 spectrometer (Varian, Japan https://www.varian.com). Chemicals shifts (δ) were specified in ppm relative to solvent residual peaks as internal standards. A Zeiss LSM 880 confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany https://www.zeiss.com.cn) was utilized for fluorescence imaging.

The Y-CDs were developed following a previously reported method [28]. Coum was synthesized according to a previously reported procedure by Robert and coworkers [29]. To obtain the nanoprobe Coum-CDs, a mixture of Y-CDs (20 mg), Coum (130 mg), 2-(7-azabenzotriazol-1-yl)-N,N,N',N'-tetramethyluronium hexafluorophosphate (HATU, 70 μL), and N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA, 115 mg) in anhydrous DMF (5 mL) was stirred at room temperature for 4 h under an argon atmosphere (Scheme 1 A). After that, the mixture was poured into brine, and the precipitate that formed as a result, was filtered off, and then purified using silica gel flash column chromatography with CH2Cl2/CH3OH (10: 1, v/v) as eluents to obtain Coum-CDs in the form of a dark brown solid.

The stock solution concentration for HSO3 − and other species was freshly prepared at 1 mM. Coum-CDs (1 mg/mL) dissolved in acetone were used as the stock solution. After the stock solution of Coum-CDs was diluted with 20 mM phosphate buffer (PB) solution (pH = 7.4, containing 2% acetone), a freshly prepared test solution of Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) was then used for spectroscopic assessments. Coum-CDs fluorescence titration with HSO3 − was performed as follows: a quartz cuvette was filled with 3 mL of 20 μg/mL Coum-CDs solution. After that, a micro-injector was used to gradually add a certain amount of HSO3 − equivalents to the nanoprobe solution. The volume introduced was not greater than 3% of the total. After the solution had been instantly mixed and incubated (within one minute), it was then excited at 350 nm. At room temperature, fluorescence emission spectra were collected from each sample using a spectrometer with a slide width of 5/5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HSO3 − detection in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sugars and wine were obtained from a supermarket (Hanzhong, China). Sugars (4.0 g), including granulated and crystal sugar, were thoroughly dissolved in 10 mL of double-distilled water before HSO3 − analysis. After being filtered, the red wine, white wine, and rice wine samples were all used immediately. The fluorescence signals of each sample were collected after adding various concentrations of HSO3 − with nanoprobe Coum-CDs (20 μg/mL) to the food samples.

HeLa cells were seeded on sterile coverslips in 6-well culture plates for 24 h before being used in experiments. For exogenous HSO3 − imaging, Coum-CDs (60 μg/mL) in RPMI medium were applied to the cells for 4 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed with PB saline (PBS, pH 7.4), and the process was carried out three times to eliminate any unbound Coum-CDs. The cells were then incubated for an additional 0.5 h at 37 °C with 1 and 10 μM HSO3 −. Cell imaging was acquired using a Zeiss LSM 880 confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany) after the cells had been washed three times with PBS. With an excitation wavelength of 405 nm, the images were recorded using a sequential line scanning model. The emission signals were measured between 450 and 490 nm for channel 1 and 510 and 650 nm for channel 2. Using ImageJ software, data were analyzed after all the images were taken under identical conditions.

HeLa cells were treated with Coum-CDs (60 μg/mL) for 4 h to test for endogenous HSO3 −. In the control group, HeLa cells loaded with Coum-CDs were given 0.5 h of treatment with a solution of Na2S2O3 (250 μM) and glutathione (GSH, 500 μM). To perform the 2,4,6-trinitrobenzenesulfonate (TNBS) inhibition test, HeLa cells loaded with Coum-CDs were first pretreated for 0.5 h with TNBS (10 mM), followed by another 0.5 h of treatment with a solution of Na2S2O3 (250 μM) and GSH (500 μM). For another comparison test, HeLa cells loaded with Coum-CDs were treated with only GSH (500 μM) for 0.5 h. Cell imaging was then performed using the conditions described above.

Nanoprobes must first be molecularly specific, and exclusive recognition between the nanoprobes and targeted molecules is thus of the utmost importance. With this in mind, our design scheme for the nanoprobe based on CDs for HSO3 − is clarified in Scheme 1B. We inferred that the detection mechanism depended on a FRET process. In this system, Y-CDs served as the FRET accepter and carrier for the system, and Coum serving as the FRET donor was selected as the recognition unit based on the following considerations: (1) HSO3 − can selectively react with the C-4 atom of Coum [30]; (2) the fluorescence emission spectrum (420–570 nm) of Coum overlaps favorably with the excitation spectrum of Y-CDs (350–500 nm) (Fig. S1), and the fluorescence emission intensity of Coum greatly decreases after reacting with HSO3 − (Fig. S2); and (3) Coum with a carboxyl group can be effortlessly bonded to the surface of Y-CDs through a condensation reaction to promote the FRET process. Most importantly, the fluorescence of the Y-CDs was not impacted by HSO3 −, even at high concentrations (Fig. S3). Thus, Coum-CDs will exhibit dual fluorescent emission at 480 and 565 nm due to the FRET process between Y-CDs and Coum. Upon treatment with HSO3 −, the π-conjugation structure of Coum was interrupted, leading to disturbance of the FRET system between Coum and Y-CDs, which resulted in dramatic changes in the absorption and fluorescence spectra. That is, a specific ratiometric nanoprobe for HSO3 − was successfully developed (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical behaviors of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental compositions and surface functional groups of Coum-CDs and Y-CDs were identified by XPS and FT-IR spectra, respectively. The full XPS spectra (Fig. 1 A) of Coum-CDs and Y-CDs showed three typical peaks at 284.7, 400.4, and 532.5 eV. The Y-CDs are comprised of C1s (74.21%), N1s (14.82%), and O1s (10.97%), and the atomic percentages of those in Coum-CDs are different from those in Y-CDs, C1s (74.49%), N1s (6.08%), and O1s (19.43%). Compared with Y-CDs, the oxygen content of Coum-CDs is largely promoted as well as a large decrease in nitrogen content. Moreover, the high-resolution spectra of C 1 s and N 1 s show that Coum-CDs and Y-CDs have different surface groups. As displayed in Fig. 1C and D, the C 1 s spectra of Y-CDs displayed three peaks, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and OC–O [31], while the C 1 s spectra of Coum-CDs could merely be deconvoluted into two peaks, and the peak of OC–O disappeared. As shown in Fig. 1E and F, the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s of Y-CDs exhibited distinctive peaks, representing pyridinic N and amino N [32], while the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s of Coum-CDs revealed a significant appearance of amide N or diethylamino N [33,34]. Furthermore, a comparison of the FTIR spectra of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs reveals two important observations (Fig. 1B). One is a decrease in the typical stretching vibration of amino bonds at 3135 cm−1 from Y-CDs to Coum-CDs. The other is that the absorption bands of –CONH− (3435 and 1539 cm−1) in Coum-CDs were detected.

Then, the morphology of Coum-CDs was performed by using TEM. As shown in Fig. S4, the size distribution of this nanosensor was estimated at 1.8–4.2 nm, with an average size of 2.9 nm. The obtained Coum-CDs was also characterized by 1HNMR (Fig. S5). In addition, the optical properties of Y-CDs and Coum-CDs were tested. As displayed in Fig. S6, the incorporation of the targeting ligand onto CDs remarkably affected the emission property of the Y-CDs, and a new peak at 480 nm was observed. In summary, these above findings strongly manifested that Coum was successfully bonded on the surface of the Y-CDs via a condensation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing property of Coum-CDs for HSO3 −
ce:section.ce:para: First, the pH effect on the fluorescence of Coum-CDs was investigated for practical application (Fig. S7). The results demonstrated that the variation in pH (from 5 to 9) does not significantly affect the fluorescence of Coum-CDs. Next, the pH effect on the probe Coum-CDs' fluorescence response to HSO3 − was examined. As shown in Fig. S7, the reaction between HSO3 − and Coum-CDs was more successful in alkaline and neutral conditions than in acidic conditions. This may be because H+ and HSO3 − are combined in these conditions. Therefore, all experiments were conducted under neutral conditions, and the concentration of the target species HSO3 − was sufficient under these conditions. Then, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 565 nm/I 480 nm) of Coum-CDs after the addition of HSO3 − was determined to investigate the response time of the nanosensor toward HSO3 −. As shown in Fig. S8, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 565 nm/I 480 nm) of Coum-CDs reached a plateau at 5 min and then remained constant.

Subsequently, the titration experiment of Coum-CDs for sensing HSO3 − was performed with UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As the concentration of HSO3 − increased, the Coum-CDs' absorption spectra decreased in the region of long wavelengths (380–500 nm), as illustrated in Fig. 2 A. Coum-CDs, on the other hand, displayed a ratiometric fluorescent response to HSO3 −, which decreased in the long wavelength region (500–700 nm) and changed slightly in the short wavelength region (450–500 nm) (Fig. 2B). These phenomena could be attributed to the fact that HSO3 − can specifically react with the C-4 atom of Coum, disturbing the entire structure's π-conjugation, as confirmed by Chang and coworkers [18]. Without HSO3 −, the Coum with the π-conjugation system could donate excited state energy to Y-CDs, and the nanoprobe Coum-CDs displayed intense yellow fluorescence. The π-conjugation system of Coum was disrupted by HSO3 −, and energy transfer from Coum (donor) to Y-CDs (acceptor) was absent. As a result, the yellow fluorescence was reduced, allowing for the selective detection of HSO3 −.

Further examination of the fluorescence titration data revealed that the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 565 nm/I 480 nm) is linear against HSO3 − variation in the ranges of 0.0–0.5 μM and 1.0–16 μM, respectively (Fig. S9A and S9B). The calibration curve was employed to determine the limit of detection of Coum-CDs for HSO3 −, which was determined to be 15 nM using the formula LOD = 3σ/k. This result implied that the sensor is capable of quantitatively detecting HSO3 − within a specific minimal concentration range. More impressively, the sensor Coum-CDs' response to HSO3 − demonstrated superior analytical performance, including higher accuracy and sensitivity, when compared to other HSO3 − detection methods that have been reported. The results are shown in Table 1
[7,26,27,35–38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study of the Coum-CD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity and interference of Coum-CDs toward HSO3 −, the fluorescence spectra of Coum-CDs were measured before and after the addition of various analytes (including Cl−, F–, Br–, SO3 2-, OH−, ONOO−, CO3 2−, NO2 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, NO3 −, SCN–, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, ClO−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−, S2O3 2–, C2O4 2–, Cr2O7 2−, S2−, H2S, H2O2, GSH, Cys, Hcy). As shown in Fig. 2C, only HSO3 − significantly altered the ratios of fluorescence intensity (I 565 nm/I 480 nm) of Coum-CDs, while other analytes had little effect. Therefore, Coum-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity for HSO3 − relative to other relevant analytes, including reactive sulfur-containing species. Competitive tests were carried out by adding HSO3 − to the aforementioned interference species-spiked Coum-CD solution to further explore the specificity of Coum-CDs (Fig. 2C). The ratiometric probe Coum-CDs displayed a similar fluorescence response to HSO3 − despite the presence of other interfering species, demonstrating the high selectivity of Coum-CDs to HSO3 −. In other words, the Coum-CDs is appropriate for accurately measuring HSO3 − in a complex system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensor Coum-CDs detect HSO3 − in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In food chemistry, HSO3 − is frequently employed as an antioxidant and preservative; however, consuming excessive amounts of HSO3 − can be harmful [39,40]. As a result, whether nanoprobe Coum-CDs could be used practically to detect HSO3 − in food samples, such as sugar and wine was investigated. As shown in Table 2 , the HSO3 − intrinsic concentrations in the three types of wine were 2.83, 3.72, and 2.58 μM, and the HSO3 − primitive levels in the two types of sugar were 2.25 and 3.38 μM, respectively. Additionally, after adding different HSO3 − concentrations to the samples of sugar and wine, the recoveries were found to be in the range of 96.81 to 101.09%, demonstrating the high detection accuracy of the Coum-CDs probe for HSO3 − in real food samples. The findings demonstrated the capability of Coum-CDs to quantitatively detect HSO3 − in real food samples and may have useful applications in food monitoring.

The results of the aforementioned experiment encouraged us to further examine the applicability of Coum-CDs for intracellular HSO3 −. The cytotoxicity of this nanoprobe was studied using the imaging application of Coum-CDs in vivo. As depicted in Fig. S10, cells treated with Coum-CDs at high concentrations (up to 60 μg/mL) did not experience a significant decrease in viability, indicating that they have very low in vitro toxicity. It was subsequently established that Coum-CDs can be used to detect exogenous HSO3 − in living cells. As shown in Fig. 3 , HeLa cells treated with Coum-CDs emitted both strong yellow and faint blue fluorescence. However, when HeLa cells were pretreated with 1 and 10 μM HSO3 − and then incubated with 60 μg/mL Coum-CDs, the blue fluorescence emission was slightly changed, and the yellow fluorescence emission was visibly reduced. The preliminary results of the cell experiment demonstrated the utility of Coum-CDs for the ratiometric detection of exogenous HSO3 − in living cells.

Consequently, it was investigated whether Coum-CDs could be used to detect endogenous HSO3 − in living cells. Equation (1) shows that thiosulfate can be used as a substrate for thiosulfate sulfurtransferase (TST), leading to the production of endogenous HSO3 − [41,42].
(1)
2 G S H +
S 2
O
3
2 -
→
TST
G S S G +
HS
-
+ H S
O
3
-

As illustrated in Fig. 4 , after incubating HeLa cells with 250.0 μM Na2S2O3 and 500.0 μM GSH for 0.5 h and then incubating them for 4 h with Coum-CDs, the relative intensity ratio of yellow and blue fluorescence significantly decreased compared to the control group that was incubated with Coum-CDs only. Fluorescence was not significantly changed after 0.5 h of incubation with 250 μM Na2S2O3 and 500 μM GSH in Coum-CD-loaded HeLa cells that had been pretreated with 10 mM TNBS (a TST inhibitor), as depicted in Fig. 4. Similarly, if Coum-CD-loaded HeLa cells were only incubated with GSH, endogenous HSO3 − should not be produced; consequently, the relative intensity ratio of yellow and blue fluorescence did not change noticeably in comparison to the control group. These findings showed that endogenous HSO3 − produced by the addition of GSH/S2O3 2− to cells could be detected by the Coum-CDs.

In conclusion, Coum-CDs were developed as a ratiometric nanoprobe for the selective detection of HSO3 − in food samples and living cells. The nucleophilic addition reaction between HSO3 − and the C-4 atom of Coum allowed the designed nanoprobe to detect HSO3 − with high selectivity and sensitivity and a low detection limit. In particular, Coum-CDs were shown to be suitable for the ratiometric imaging of endogenous and exogenous HSO3 − in living cells as well as the quantitative determination of HSO3 − in food samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of very few reports of a CD-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for the detection of HSO3 −, and we anticipate that the results of this work will pave the way for the development of additional analyte-specific ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes.

Qin Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yanli Sun: Data curation, Investigation, Validation. Ju Ge: Investigation, Methodology. Lihua Li: Investigation, Validation. Jiufu Lu: . Dan Zhang: . Lingxia Jin: Investigation, Supervision, Resources. Hua Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Shengrui Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22177066), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2021KJXX-51), the Educational Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Province of China (20JK0575), and the Co-construction Project of Hanzhong city and Shaanxi University of Technology (SXJ-2103, SXJ-2105), the Youth Innovative Team of Shaanxi Universities [No.2022.94].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108561.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
